A **bank** is a financial institution that accepts [deposits](/wiki/Deposit_account "Deposit account") from the public and creates a [demand deposit](/wiki/Demand_deposit "Demand deposit") while making [loans](/wiki/Loan "Loan").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Lending activities can be directly performed by the bank or indirectly through [capital markets](/wiki/Capital_market "Capital market").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Banks play an important role in financial stability and the economy of a country, so most countries exercise a high degree of [regulation over banks](/wiki/Bank_regulation "Bank regulation"). Most countries have institutionalized a system known as [fractional-reserve banking](/wiki/Fractional-reserve_banking "Fractional-reserve banking"), under which banks hold liquid [assets](/wiki/Asset "Asset") equal to only a portion of their [current liabilities](/wiki/Current_liability "Current liability").[[3]](#cite_note-3) In addition to other regulations intended to ensure [liquidity](/wiki/Accounting_liquidity "Accounting liquidity"), banks are generally subject to [minimum capital requirements](/wiki/Minimum_capital_requirement "Minimum capital requirement") based on an international set of capital standards, the [Basel Accords](/wiki/Basel_Accords "Basel Accords").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Banking in its modern sense evolved in the fourteenth century in the prosperous cities of [Renaissance Italy](/wiki/Renaissance_Italy "Renaissance Italy") but, in many ways, functioned as a continuation of ideas and concepts of [credit](/wiki/Credit_(finance) "Credit (finance)") and [lending](/wiki/Lending "Lending") that have their roots in the ancient world. In the [history of banking](/wiki/History_of_banking "History of banking"), a number of [banking dynasties](/wiki/List_of_banking_families "List of banking families") – notably, the [Medicis](/wiki/House_of_Medici "House of Medici"), the [Pazzi](/wiki/Pazzi "Pazzi"), the [Fuggers](/wiki/Fugger "Fugger"), the [Welsers](/wiki/Welser "Welser"), the [Berenbergs](/wiki/Berenberg_family "Berenberg family"), and the [Rothschilds](/wiki/Rothschild_family "Rothschild family") – have played a central role over many centuries. The [oldest existing](/wiki/List_of_oldest_banks_in_continuous_operation "List of oldest banks in continuous operation") [retail bank](/wiki/Retail_bank "Retail bank") is [Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena](/wiki/Banca_Monte_dei_Paschi_di_Siena "Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena") (founded in 1472), while the oldest existing [merchant bank](/wiki/Merchant_bank "Merchant bank") is [Berenberg Bank](/wiki/Berenberg_Bank "Berenberg Bank") (founded in 1590).

A **banker** is a person who engages in the business of banking.

## Activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Activities")]

### Standard business

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Standard business")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/WinonaSavingsBankVault.JPG/250px-WinonaSavingsBankVault.JPG)](/wiki/File:WinonaSavingsBankVault.JPG)

Large door to an old [bank vault](/wiki/Bank_vault "Bank vault").

Banks act as payment agents by conducting [checking or current accounts](/wiki/Transactional_account "Transactional account") for customers, paying checks drawn by customers in the bank, and collecting checks deposited to customers' current accounts. Banks also enable customer payments via other payment methods such as [Automated Clearing House](/wiki/Automated_Clearing_House "Automated Clearing House") (ACH), [Wire transfers](/wiki/Wire_transfer "Wire transfer") or [telegraphic transfer](/wiki/Telegraphic_transfer "Telegraphic transfer"), [EFTPOS](/wiki/EFTPOS "EFTPOS"), and [automated teller machines](/wiki/Automated_teller_machine "Automated teller machine") (ATMs).

Banks borrow money by accepting funds deposited on current accounts, by accepting [term deposits](/wiki/Term_deposit "Term deposit"), and by issuing debt securities such as [banknotes](/wiki/Banknotes "Banknotes") and [bonds](/wiki/Bond_(finance) "Bond (finance)"). Banks lend money by making advances to customers on current accounts, by making [installment loans](/wiki/Installment_loan "Installment loan"), and by investing in marketable debt securities and other forms of money lending.

Banks provide different payment services, and a bank account is considered indispensable by most businesses and individuals. Nonbanks that provide payment services such as remittance companies are normally not considered as an adequate substitute for a bank account.

Banks issue new money when they make loans. In contemporary banking systems, regulators set a minimum level of reserve funds that banks must hold against the deposit liabilities created by the funding of these loans, in order to ensure that the banks can meet demands for payment of such deposits. These reserves can be acquired through the acceptance of new deposits, sale of other assets, or borrowing from other banks including the central bank.[[5]](#cite_note-money-creation-5)

### Range of activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Range of activities")]

Activities undertaken by banks include [personal banking](/wiki/Retail_banking "Retail banking"), [corporate banking](/wiki/Commercial_bank "Commercial bank"), [investment banking](/wiki/Investment_banking "Investment banking"), [private banking](/wiki/Private_banking "Private banking"), [transaction banking](/wiki/Transaction_banking "Transaction banking"), [insurance](/wiki/Insurance "Insurance"), [consumer finance](/wiki/Loan "Loan"), [trade finance](/wiki/Trade_finance "Trade finance") and other related.

### Channels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Channels")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/SunTrust_Bank%2C_Gaithersburg%2C_Maryland%2C_August_25%2C_2015.jpg/250px-SunTrust_Bank%2C_Gaithersburg%2C_Maryland%2C_August_25%2C_2015.jpg)](/wiki/File:SunTrust_Bank,_Gaithersburg,_Maryland,_August_25,_2015.jpg)

An American bank in Maryland.

Banks offer many different channels to access their banking and other services:

* [Branch, in-person banking](/wiki/Branch_(banking) "Branch (banking)") in a retail location
* [Automated teller machine banking](/wiki/Automated_teller_machine "Automated teller machine") adjacent to or remote from the bank
* Bank by mail: Most banks accept check deposits via mail and use mail to communicate to their customers
* [Online banking](/wiki/Online_banking "Online banking") over the Internet to perform multiple types of transactions
* [Mobile banking](/wiki/Mobile_banking "Mobile banking") is using one's mobile phone to conduct banking transactions
* [Telephone banking](/wiki/Telephone_banking "Telephone banking") allows customers to conduct transactions over the telephone with an [automated attendant](/wiki/Automated_attendant "Automated attendant"), or when requested, with a [telephone operator](/wiki/Switchboard_operator "Switchboard operator")
* [Video banking](/wiki/Video_banking "Video banking") performs banking transactions or professional banking consultations via a remote video and audio connection. Video banking can be performed via purpose built banking transaction machines (similar to an Automated teller machine) or via a [video conference](/wiki/Video_conference "Video conference") enabled bank branch clarification
* [Relationship manager](/wiki/Customer_relationship_management "Customer relationship management"), mostly for private banking or business banking, who visits customers at their homes or businesses
* [Direct Selling Agent](/wiki/Direct_Selling_Association "Direct Selling Association"), who works for the bank based on a contract, whose main job is to increase the customer base for the bank

### Business models

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Business models")]

Banks generate revenue in a variety of different ways including interest, transaction fees and financial advice. Traditionally, the most significant method is via charging [interest](/wiki/Interest "Interest") on the capital it lends out to customers.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The bank [profits](/wiki/Profit_(economics) "Profit (economics)") from the difference between the level of interest it pays for deposits and other sources of funds, and the level of interest it charges in its lending activities.

This difference is referred to as the [spread](/wiki/Net_interest_spread "Net interest spread") between the cost of funds and the loan interest rate. Historically, profitability from lending activities has been cyclical and dependent on the needs and strengths of loan customers and the stage of the [economic cycle](/wiki/Economic_cycle "Economic cycle"). Fees and financial advice constitute a more stable revenue stream and banks have therefore placed more emphasis on these revenue lines to smooth their financial performance.

In the past 20 years, American banks have taken many measures to ensure that they remain profitable while responding to increasingly changing market conditions.

* First, this includes the [Gramm–Leach–Bliley Act](/wiki/Gramm%E2%80%93Leach%E2%80%93Bliley_Act "Gramm–Leach–Bliley Act"), which allows banks again to merge with investment and insurance houses. Merging banking, investment, and insurance functions allows traditional banks to respond to increasing consumer demands for "one-stop shopping" by enabling [cross-selling](/wiki/Cross-selling "Cross-selling") of products (which, the banks hope, will also increase profitability).
* Second, they have expanded the use of [risk-based pricing](/wiki/Risk-based_pricing "Risk-based pricing") from business lending to consumer lending, which means charging higher interest rates to those customers that are considered to be a higher credit risk and thus increased chance of [default](/wiki/Default_(finance) "Default (finance)") on loans. This helps to offset the losses from bad loans, lowers the price of loans to those who have better credit histories, and offers credit products to high risk customers who would otherwise be denied credit.
* Third, they have sought to increase the methods of payment processing available to the general public and business clients. These products include [debit cards](/wiki/Debit_card "Debit card"), prepaid cards, [smart cards](/wiki/Smart_card "Smart card"), and [credit cards](/wiki/Credit_card "Credit card"). They make it easier for consumers to conveniently make transactions and [smooth their consumption](/wiki/Consumption_smoothing "Consumption smoothing") over time (in some countries with underdeveloped financial systems, it is still common to deal strictly in cash, including carrying suitcases filled with cash to purchase a home).

:   However, with the convenience of easy credit, there is also an increased risk that consumers will mismanage their financial resources and accumulate excessive debt. Banks make money from card products through interest charges and fees charged to credit and debit card holders, and [transaction fees](/wiki/Transaction_fee "Transaction fee") to retailers[[7]](#cite_note-7) who accept the bank's cards for payments.

This helps in making a profit and facilitates economic development as a whole.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Recently, as banks have been faced with pressure from fintechs, new and additional business models have been suggested such as freemium, monetization of data, white-labeling of banking and payment applications, or the cross-selling of complementary products.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of banking](/wiki/History_of_banking "History of banking")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Wypedzenie.jpg/250px-Wypedzenie.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wypedzenie.jpg)

This 15th-century painting depicts money-dealers at a *banca* (bench) during the [Cleansing of the Temple](/wiki/Cleansing_of_the_Temple "Cleansing of the Temple").

Banking as an archaic activity (or quasi-banking[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)) is thought to have begun as early as the end of the 4th millennium BCE,[[12]](#cite_note-12) to the 3rd millennia BCE.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Medieval

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Medieval")]

In Europe, the first recorded instances of private banks were run by the [Knights Templar](/wiki/Knights_Templar "Knights Templar"). The Knights provided safe passage for pilgrims traveling to Jerusalem. To avoid being targeted by robbers because of the large sum of [money](/wiki/Money "Money") required for the pilgrimage, pilgrims would exchange money in Templar strongholds for a [receipt](/wiki/Receipt "Receipt"). They could then withdraw the money along the route at other Templar strongholds to purchase necessities.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The organization would provide these services from the 12th century until their disbandment in the early 14th century.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The present era of banking can be traced to wealthy medieval [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") Italian city-states, whose elite families such as the [Bardi](/wiki/Bardi_family "Bardi family") and [Peruzzi](/wiki/Peruzzi "Peruzzi") dominated banking in 14th-century Florence before establishing branches in many other parts of Europe.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] [Giovanni di Bicci de' Medici](/wiki/Giovanni_di_Bicci_de%27_Medici "Giovanni di Bicci de' Medici") set up one of the most famous Italian banks, the [Medici Bank](/wiki/Medici_Bank "Medici Bank"), in 1397.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The [Consell de Cent](/wiki/Consell_de_Cent "Consell de Cent") founded the earliest-known state deposit bank, the [Taula de canvi de Barcelona](/wiki/Taula_de_canvi_de_Barcelona "Taula de canvi de Barcelona") (Table of Change), in 1401 at [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona"). This was followed by The [Bank of Saint George](/wiki/Bank_of_Saint_George "Bank of Saint George"), created in 1407 at [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa"), Italy.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Early modern

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Early modern")]

Further information: [History of banking in the United Kingdom](/wiki/History_of_banking_in_the_United_Kingdom "History of banking in the United Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Bank_of_England_Charter_sealing_1694.jpg/250px-Bank_of_England_Charter_sealing_1694.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bank_of_England_Charter_sealing_1694.jpg)

*Sealing of the [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England") Charter (1694)*, by Lady Jane Lindsay, 1905.

[Fractional reserve banking](/wiki/Fractional_reserve_banking "Fractional reserve banking") and the issue of [banknotes](/wiki/Banknote "Banknote") emerged in the 17th and 18th centuries. Merchants started to store their gold with the [goldsmiths](/wiki/Goldsmith "Goldsmith") of [London](/wiki/London "London"), who possessed private [vaults](/wiki/Bank_vault "Bank vault"), and who charged a fee for that service. In exchange for each deposit of precious metal, the goldsmiths issued [receipts](/wiki/Receipt "Receipt") certifying the quantity and purity of the metal they held as a [bailee](/wiki/Bailee "Bailee"); these receipts could not be assigned, only the original depositor could collect the stored goods.

Gradually the goldsmiths began to lend money out on behalf of the [depositor](/wiki/Deposit_(finance) "Deposit (finance)"), and [promissory notes](/wiki/Promissory_note "Promissory note"), which evolved into banknotes, were issued for money deposited as a loan to the goldsmith. By the 19th century, ordinary deposits of money in banks had become a mere loan, or *mutuum*, and the bank restored an equivalent sum whenever it was demanded[[20]](#cite_note-20)
Money, when paid into a bank, ceases altogether to be the money of the principal (see Parker v. Marchant, 1 Phillips 360); it is then the money of the banker, who is bound to return an equivalent, by paying a similar sum to that deposited with him, when he is asked for it.
[[21]](#cite_note-21)
The goldsmith paid interest on deposits. Since the promissory notes were payable on demand, and the advances (loans) to the goldsmith's customers were repayable over a longer time-period, this was an early form of [fractional reserve banking](/wiki/Fractional_reserve_banking "Fractional reserve banking"). The promissory notes developed into an assignable instrument which could circulate as a safe and convenient form of money[[22]](#cite_note-22)
backed by the goldsmith's promise to pay,[[23]](#cite_note-23)
allowing goldsmiths to advance loans with little risk of [default](/wiki/Default_(finance) "Default (finance)").[[24]](#cite_note-24) Thus the goldsmiths of London became the forerunners of banking by creating new money based on credit.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Pohjoismaiden_Osakepankin_Helsingin_sivukonttori.jpg/250px-Pohjoismaiden_Osakepankin_Helsingin_sivukonttori.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pohjoismaiden_Osakepankin_Helsingin_sivukonttori.jpg)

Interior of the [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki") Branch of the [Vyborg-Bank](/w/index.php?title=Vyborg-Bank&action=edit&redlink=1 "Vyborg-Bank (page does not exist)") [[fi](https://fi.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pohjoismaiden_Osakepankki "fi:Pohjoismaiden Osakepankki")] in the 1910s

The [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England") originated the permanent issue of banknotes in 1695.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The [Royal Bank of Scotland](/wiki/Royal_Bank_of_Scotland "Royal Bank of Scotland") established the first [overdraft](/wiki/Overdraft "Overdraft") facility in 1728.[[26]](#cite_note-26) By the beginning of the 19th century, [Lubbock's Bank](/wiki/Sir_John_Lubbock,_2nd_Baronet "Sir John Lubbock, 2nd Baronet") had established a [bankers' clearing house](/wiki/Bankers%27_clearing_house "Bankers' clearing house") in London to allow multiple banks to clear transactions. The [Rothschilds](/wiki/Rothschild_family "Rothschild family") pioneered [international finance](/wiki/International_finance "International finance") on a large scale,[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) financing the purchase of shares in the [Suez canal](/wiki/Suez_canal "Suez canal") for the British government in 1875.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[*[need quotation to verify](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

### Modern

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Modern")]

During the 20th century, developments in telecommunications and computing caused major changes to banks' operations and let banks dramatically increase in size and geographic spread. The [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis") led to [bank failures](/wiki/Bank_failure "Bank failure"), including some of the world's largest banks, and provoked debate about [bank regulation](/wiki/Bank_regulation "Bank regulation").

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The word *bank* was taken into [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English") from [Middle French](/wiki/Middle_French "Middle French") *banque*, from Old [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") *banco*, meaning "table", from [Old High German](/wiki/Old_High_German "Old High German") *banc, bank* "bench, counter". Benches were used as makeshift desks or exchange counters during the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") by [Florentine](/wiki/Florence "Florence") bankers, who used to make their transactions atop desks covered by green tablecloths.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Definition")]

The definition of a bank varies from country to country. See the relevant country pages for more information.

Under [English common law](/wiki/English_common_law "English common law"), a banker is defined as a person who carries on the business of banking by conducting [current accounts](/wiki/Current_account_(banking) "Current account (banking)") for their customers, paying [checks](/wiki/Check_(financial_instrument) "Check (financial instrument)") drawn on them as well as collecting them for their customers.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Banco_de_Venezuela%2C_Coro.JPG/250px-Banco_de_Venezuela%2C_Coro.JPG)](/wiki/File:Banco_de_Venezuela,_Coro.JPG)

[Banco de Venezuela](/wiki/Banco_de_Venezuela "Banco de Venezuela") in [Coro](/wiki/Coro,_Venezuela "Coro, Venezuela").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Bank.Pokhara.JPG/250px-Bank.Pokhara.JPG)](/wiki/File:Bank.Pokhara.JPG)

Branch of [Nepal Bank](/wiki/Nepal_Bank_Limited "Nepal Bank Limited") in Pokhara, Western Nepal.

In most common law jurisdictions there is a [Bills of Exchange](/wiki/Bills_of_Exchange "Bills of Exchange") Act that codifies the law in relation to [negotiable instruments](/wiki/Negotiable_instruments "Negotiable instruments"), including checks, and this Act contains a statutory definition of the term *banker*: *banker* includes a body of persons, whether incorporated or not, who carry on the business of banking' (Section 2, Interpretation). Although this definition seems circular, it is actually functional, because it ensures that the legal basis for bank transactions such as checks does not depend on how the bank is structured or regulated.

The business of banking is in many common law countries not defined by statute but by common law, the definition above. In other English common law jurisdictions there are statutory definitions of the *business of banking* or *banking business*. When looking at these definitions it is important to keep in mind that they are defining the business of banking for the purposes of the legislation, and not necessarily in general. In particular, most of the definitions are from legislation that has the purpose of regulating and supervising banks rather than regulating the actual business of banking. However, in many cases, the statutory definition closely mirrors the common law one. Examples of statutory definitions:

* "banking business" means the business of receiving money on current or deposit account, paying and collecting checks drawn by or paid in by customers, the making of advances to customers, and includes such other business as the Authority may prescribe for the purposes of this Act; (Banking Act ([Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore")), Section 2, Interpretation).
* "banking business" means the business of either or both of the following:

1. receiving from the general public money on current, deposit, savings or other similar account repayable on demand or within less than [3 months] ... or with a period of call or notice of less than that period;
2. paying or collecting checks drawn by or paid in by customers.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Since the advent of [EFTPOS](/wiki/EFTPOS "EFTPOS") (Electronic Funds Transfer at Point Of Sale), direct credit, [direct debit](/wiki/Direct_debit "Direct debit") and [internet banking](/wiki/Online_banking "Online banking"), the check has lost its primacy in most banking systems as a payment instrument. This has led legal theorists to suggest that the check based definition should be broadened to include financial institutions that conduct current accounts for customers and enable customers to pay and be paid by third parties, even if they do not pay and collect checks .[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Banks face a number of [risks](/wiki/Financial_risk "Financial risk") in order to conduct their business, and how well these risks are managed and understood is a key driver behind profitability, and how much [capital](/wiki/Capital_requirement "Capital requirement") a bank is required to hold. Bank capital consists principally of [equity](/wiki/Common_stock "Common stock"), [retained earnings](/wiki/Retained_earnings "Retained earnings") and [subordinated debt](/wiki/Subordinated_debt "Subordinated debt").

Some of the main risks faced by banks include:

* [Credit risk](/wiki/Credit_risk "Credit risk"): risk of loss arising from a borrower who does not make payments as promised.[[35]](#cite_note-35)
* [Liquidity risk](/wiki/Liquidity_risk "Liquidity risk"): risk that a given security or asset cannot be traded quickly enough in the market to prevent a loss (or make the required profit).
* [Market risk](/wiki/Market_risk "Market risk"): risk that the value of a portfolio, either an investment portfolio or a trading portfolio, will decrease due to the change in value of the market risk factors.
* [Operational risk](/wiki/Operational_risk "Operational risk"): risk arising from the execution of a company's business functions.
* [Reputational risk](/wiki/Reputational_risk "Reputational risk"): a type of risk related to the trustworthiness of the business.
* [Macroeconomic risk](/wiki/Macroeconomics "Macroeconomics"): risks related to the aggregate economy the bank is operating in.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

The [capital requirement](/wiki/Capital_requirement "Capital requirement") is a [bank regulation](/wiki/Bank_regulation "Bank regulation"), which sets a framework within which a bank or depository institution must manage its [balance sheet](/wiki/Balance_sheet "Balance sheet"). The categorization of assets and capital is highly standardized so that it can be [risk weighted](/wiki/Risk-weighted_asset "Risk-weighted asset").

After the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"), regulators force banks to issue *[Contingent convertible bonds](/wiki/Contingent_convertible_bond "Contingent convertible bond")* (CoCos). These are hybrid capital securities that absorb losses in accordance with their [contractual terms](/wiki/Contractual_term "Contractual term") when the capital of the issuing bank falls below a certain level. Then debt is reduced and bank capitalization gets a boost. Owing to their capacity to absorb losses, CoCos have the potential to satisfy regulatory capital requirement.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

## Banks in the economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Banks in the economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/SEB_Building_in_Tallinn.jpg/250px-SEB_Building_in_Tallinn.jpg)](/wiki/File:SEB_Building_in_Tallinn.jpg)

See also: [Financial system](/wiki/Financial_system "Financial system")

### Economic functions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Economic functions")]

The economic functions of banks include:

1. Issue of money, in the form of [banknotes](/wiki/Banknotes "Banknotes") and current accounts subject to check or payment at the customer's order. These claims on banks can act as money because they are negotiable or repayable on demand, and hence valued at par. They are effectively transferable by mere delivery, in the case of banknotes, or by drawing a check that the payee may bank or cash.
2. Netting and settlement of payments – banks act as both collection and paying agents for customers, participating in interbank clearing and settlement systems to collect, present, be presented with, and pay payment instruments. This enables banks to economize on reserves held for settlement of payments since inward and outward payments offset each other. It also enables the offsetting of payment flows between geographical areas, reducing the cost of settlement between them.
3. Credit quality improvement – banks lend money to ordinary commercial and personal borrowers (ordinary credit quality), but are high quality borrowers. The improvement comes from diversification of the bank's assets and capital which provides a buffer to absorb losses without defaulting on its obligations. However, banknotes and deposits are generally unsecured; if the bank gets into difficulty and pledges assets as security, to raise the funding it needs to continue to operate, this puts the note holders and depositors in an economically subordinated position.
4. [Asset–liability mismatch](/wiki/Asset%E2%80%93liability_mismatch "Asset–liability mismatch")/[Maturity transformation](/wiki/Maturity_transformation "Maturity transformation") – banks borrow more on demand debt and short term debt, but provide more long-term loans. In other words, they borrow short and lend long. With a stronger credit quality than most other borrowers, banks can do this by aggregating issues (e.g. accepting deposits and issuing banknotes) and redemptions (e.g. withdrawals and redemption of banknotes), maintaining reserves of cash, investing in marketable securities that can be readily converted to cash if needed, and raising replacement funding as needed from various sources (e.g. wholesale cash markets and securities markets).
5. [Money creation](/wiki/Money_creation "Money creation")/destruction – whenever a bank gives out a loan in a [fractional-reserve banking](/wiki/Fractional-reserve_banking "Fractional-reserve banking") system, a new sum of money is created and conversely, whenever the principal on that loan is repaid money is destroyed.

## Bank crisis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Bank crisis")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Bosakov_dom.jpg/250px-Bosakov_dom.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bosakov_dom.jpg)

OTP Bank in [Prešov](/wiki/Pre%C5%A1ov "Prešov") (Slovakia)

Banks are susceptible to many forms of risk which have triggered occasional systemic crises.[[39]](#cite_note-39) These include [liquidity risk](/wiki/Liquidity_risk "Liquidity risk") (where many depositors request withdrawals in excess of available funds), [credit risk](/wiki/Credit_risk "Credit risk") (the chance that those who owe money to the bank will not repay it), and [interest rate risk](/wiki/Interest_rate_risk "Interest rate risk") (the possibility that the bank will become unprofitable, if rising interest rates force it to pay relatively more on its deposits than it receives on its loans).

Bank crises have developed many times throughout history when one or more risks have emerged for the banking sector as a whole. Prominent examples include the [bank run](/wiki/Bank_run "Bank run") that occurred during the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), the U.S. [Savings and Loan crisis](/wiki/Savings_and_Loan_crisis "Savings and Loan crisis") in the 1980s and early 1990s, the Japanese banking crisis during the 1990s, and the [sub-prime mortgage crisis](/wiki/Sub-prime_mortgage_crisis "Sub-prime mortgage crisis") in the 2000s.

The 2023 global banking crisis is the latest of these crises: In March 2023, liquidity shortages and bank insolvencies led to [three bank failures in the United States](/wiki/March_2023_United_States_bank_failures "March 2023 United States bank failures"), and within two weeks, several of the world's largest banks failed or were shut down by regulators.

### Size of global banking industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Size of global banking industry")]

Assets of the largest 1,000 banks in the world grew by 6.8% in the 2008–2009 financial year to a record US$96.4 trillion while profits declined by 85% to US$115 billion. Growth in assets in adverse market conditions was largely a result of recapitalization. EU banks held the largest share of the total, 56% in 2008–2009, down from 61% in the previous year. Asian banks' share increased from 12% to 14% during the year, while the share of US banks increased from 11% to 13%. Fee [revenue](/wiki/Revenue "Revenue") generated by global [investment](/wiki/Investment "Investment") in banking totaled US$66.3 billion in 2009, up 12% on the previous year.[[40]](#cite_note-ifsl-40)

The United States has the most banks in the world in terms of institutions (5,330 as of 2015) and possibly branches (81,607 as of 2015).[[41]](#cite_note-41) This is an indicator of the geography and regulatory structure of the US, resulting in a large number of small to medium-sized institutions in its banking system.

As of November 2009, China's top four banks have over 67,000 branches ([ICBC](/wiki/ICBC "ICBC"):18000+, [BOC](/wiki/Bank_of_China "Bank of China"):12000+, [CCB](/wiki/China_Construction_Bank "China Construction Bank"):13000+, [ABC](/wiki/Agricultural_Bank_of_China "Agricultural Bank of China"):24000+) with 140 smaller banks with an undetermined number of branches.
Japan had 129 banks and 12,000 branches. In 2004, Germany, France, and Italy each had more than 30,000 branches – more than double the 15,000 branches in the United Kingdom.[[40]](#cite_note-ifsl-40)

Currently, commercial banks are regulated in most jurisdictions by government entities and require a special bank license to operate.

Usually, the definition of the business of banking for the purposes of regulation is extended to include acceptance of deposits, even if they are not repayable to the customer's order – although money lending, by itself, is generally not included in the definition.

Unlike most other regulated industries, the regulator is typically also a participant in the market, being either publicly or privately governed [central bank](/wiki/Central_bank "Central bank"). Central banks also typically have a monopoly on the business of issuing [banknotes](/wiki/Banknote "Banknote"). However, in some countries, this is not the case. In the UK, for example, the [Financial Services Authority](/wiki/Financial_Services_Authority "Financial Services Authority") licenses banks, and some commercial banks (such as the [Bank of Scotland](/wiki/Bank_of_Scotland "Bank of Scotland")) issue their own banknotes in addition to those issued by the [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England"), the UK government's central bank.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Basel_-_Bank_f%C3%BCr_internationalen_Zahlungsausgleich1.jpg/250px-Basel_-_Bank_f%C3%BCr_internationalen_Zahlungsausgleich1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Basel_-_Bank_f%C3%BCr_internationalen_Zahlungsausgleich1.jpg)

Global headquarters of the [Bank for International Settlements](/wiki/Bank_for_International_Settlements "Bank for International Settlements") in [Basel](/wiki/Basel "Basel")

Banking law is based on a contractual analysis of the relationship between the *bank* (defined above) and the *customer* – defined as any entity for which the bank agrees to conduct an account.

The law implies rights and obligations into this relationship as follows:

* The bank account balance is the financial position between the bank and the customer: when the account is in credit, the bank owes the balance to the customer; when the account is overdrawn, the customer owes the balance to the bank.
* The bank agrees to pay the customer's checks up to the amount standing to the credit of the customer's account, plus any agreed overdraft limit.
* The bank may not pay from the customer's account without a mandate from the customer, e.g. a check drawn by the customer.
* The bank agrees to promptly collect the checks deposited to the customer's account as the customer's agent and to credit the proceeds to the customer's account.
* And, the bank has a right to combine the customer's accounts since each account is just an aspect of the same credit relationship.
* The bank has a [lien](/wiki/Lien "Lien") on checks deposited to the customer's account, to the extent that the customer is indebted to the bank.
* The bank must not disclose details of transactions through the customer's account – unless the customer consents, there is a public duty to disclose, the bank's interests require it, or the law demands it.
* The bank must not [debank](/wiki/Debanking "Debanking") a customer without reasonable notice as regulation demands.

These implied contractual terms may be modified by express agreement between the customer and the bank. The statutes and regulations in force within a particular jurisdiction may also modify the above terms or create new rights, obligations, or limitations relevant to the bank-customer relationship.

Some types of financial institutions, such as [building societies](/wiki/Building_Society "Building Society") and [credit unions](/wiki/Credit_unions "Credit unions"), may be partly or wholly exempt from bank license requirements, and therefore regulated under separate rules.

The requirements for the issue of a bank license vary between jurisdictions but typically include:

* Minimum capital
* Minimum capital ratio
* 'Fit and Proper' requirements for the bank's controllers, owners, directors, or senior officers
* Approval of the bank's business plan as being sufficiently prudent and plausible.

## Different types of banking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Different types of banking")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Toledo%2C_city_of_opportunity-_a_chronicle_of_the_Northwest_territory_and_its_conquest_and_of_the_gracious_city_stout_hearts_and_willing_hands_have_built_-_DPLA_-_7eb9ebf857dee23e40e999198de308c4_%28page_23%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toledo,_city_of_opportunity-_a_chronicle_of_the_Northwest_territory_and_its_conquest_and_of_the_gracious_city_stout_hearts_and_willing_hands_have_built_-_DPLA_-_7eb9ebf857dee23e40e999198de308c4_(page_23)_(cropped).jpg)

An illustration of Northern National Bank as advertized in a 1921 book highlighting the opportunities available in [Toledo, Ohio](/wiki/Toledo,_Ohio "Toledo, Ohio")

Banks' activities can be divided into:

* [retail banking](/wiki/Retail_banking "Retail banking"), dealing directly with individuals and small businesses;
* [business banking](/wiki/Business_banking "Business banking"), providing services to mid-market business;
* [corporate banking](/wiki/Corporate_banking "Corporate banking"), directed at large business entities;
* [private banking](/wiki/Private_banking "Private banking"), providing wealth management services to [high-net-worth individuals](/wiki/High-net-worth_individual "High-net-worth individual") and families;
* [investment banking](/wiki/Investment_banking "Investment banking"), relating to activities on the [financial markets](/wiki/Financial_markets "Financial markets").

Most banks are profitmaking private enterprises. However, some are owned by the government, or are [nonprofits](/wiki/Nonprofit_organization "Nonprofit organization").

* [Commercial banks](/wiki/Commercial_bank "Commercial bank"): the term used for a normal bank to distinguish it from an investment bank. After the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), the U.S. Congress required that banks only engage in banking activities, whereas investment banks were limited to [capital market](/wiki/Capital_market "Capital market") activities. Since the two no longer have to be under separate ownership, some use the term "commercial bank" to refer to a bank or a division of a bank that mostly deals with deposits and loans from corporations or large businesses.
* [Community banks](/wiki/Community_banks "Community banks"): locally operated financial institutions that empower employees to make local decisions to serve their customers and partners.
* [Community development banks](/wiki/Community_development_bank "Community development bank"): regulated banks that provide financial services and credit to under-served markets or populations.
* [Land development banks](/wiki/Land_development_bank "Land development bank"): The special banks providing long-term loans are called [land development banks](/wiki/Land_development_bank "Land development bank") (LDB). The history of LDB is quite old. The first LDB was started at Jhang in [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab "Punjab") in 1920. The main objective of the LDBs is to promote the development of land, agriculture and increase the agricultural production. The LDBs provide long-term finance to members directly through their branches.[[43]](#cite_note-agritech-43)
* [Credit unions](/wiki/Credit_union "Credit union") or [co-operative banks](/wiki/Cooperative_banking "Cooperative banking"): not-for-profit [cooperatives](/wiki/Cooperatives "Cooperatives") owned by the depositors and often offering rates more favorable than for-profit banks. Typically, membership is restricted to employees of a particular company, residents of a defined area, members of a certain [union](/wiki/Labor_union "Labor union") or religious organizations, and their immediate families.
* [Postal savings banks](/wiki/Postal_savings_system "Postal savings system"): savings banks associated with national postal systems.
* [Private banks](/wiki/Private_banking "Private banking"): banks that manage the assets of high-net-worth individuals. Historically a minimum of US$1 million was required to open an account, however, over the last years, many private banks have lowered their entry hurdles to US$350,000 for private investors.[[44]](#cite_note-44)
* [Offshore banks](/wiki/Offshore_bank "Offshore bank"): banks located in jurisdictions with low taxation and regulation. Many offshore banks are essentially private banks.
* [Savings banks](/wiki/Savings_bank "Savings bank"): in Europe, savings banks took their roots in the 19th or sometimes even in the 18th century. Their original objective was to provide easily accessible savings products to all strata of the population. In some countries, savings banks were created on public initiative; in others, socially committed individuals created foundations to put in place the necessary infrastructure. Nowadays, European savings banks have kept their focus on retail banking: payments, savings products, credits, and insurances for individuals or small and medium-sized enterprises. Apart from this retail focus, they also differ from commercial banks by their broadly decentralized distribution network, providing local and regional outreach – and by their socially responsible approach to business and society.
* [Ethical banks](/wiki/Ethical_bank "Ethical bank"): banks that prioritize the transparency of all operations and make only what they consider to be socially responsible investments.
* A [direct or internet-only bank](/wiki/Direct_bank "Direct bank") is a banking operation without any physical bank branches. Transactions are usually accomplished using [ATMs](/wiki/Automated_teller_machine "Automated teller machine") and [electronic transfers](/wiki/Electronic_funds_transfer "Electronic funds transfer") and [direct deposits](/wiki/Direct_deposit "Direct deposit") through an online interface.

### Types of investment banks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Types of investment banks")]

* [Investment banks](/wiki/Investment_bank "Investment bank") "[underwrite](/wiki/Underwrite "Underwrite")" (guarantee the sale of) stock and bond issues, provide [investment management](/wiki/Investment_management "Investment management"), and advize corporations on [capital market](/wiki/Capital_market "Capital market") activities such as M&A, trade for their own accounts, make markets, provide [securities services](/w/index.php?title=Securities_Services&action=edit&redlink=1 "Securities Services (page does not exist)") to institutional clients.
* [Merchant banks](/wiki/Merchant_bank "Merchant bank") were traditionally banks which engaged in [trade finance](/wiki/Trade_finance "Trade finance"). The modern definition, however, refers to banks which provide capital to firms in the form of shares rather than loans. Unlike [venture caps](/w/index.php?title=Venture_cap&action=edit&redlink=1 "Venture cap (page does not exist)"), they tend not to invest in new companies.

### Combination banks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Combination banks")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Avenida_Sao_Joao%2C_Sao_Paulo_2006.jpg/250px-Avenida_Sao_Joao%2C_Sao_Paulo_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Avenida_Sao_Joao,_Sao_Paulo_2006.jpg)

A [Banco do Brasil](/wiki/Banco_do_Brasil "Banco do Brasil") office in [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo"), Brazil, the bank is the largest financial institution in Brazil and [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America").

* [Universal banks](/wiki/Universal_bank "Universal bank"), more commonly known as [financial services](/wiki/Financial_services "Financial services") companies, engage in several of these activities. These big banks are very diversified groups that, among other services, also distribute insurance – hence the term [bancassurance](/wiki/Bancassurance "Bancassurance"), a [portmanteau word](/wiki/Portmanteau "Portmanteau") combining "banque or bank" and "assurance", signifying that both banking and insurance are provided by the same corporate entity.

### Other types of banks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Other types of banks")]

* [Central banks](/wiki/Central_bank "Central bank") are normally government-owned and charged with quasi-regulatory responsibilities, such as supervising commercial banks, or controlling the cash [interest rate](/wiki/Interest_rate "Interest rate"). They generally provide liquidity to the banking system and act as the [lender of last resort](/wiki/Lender_of_last_resort "Lender of last resort") in event of a crisis.
* [Islamic banks](/wiki/Islamic_bank "Islamic bank") adhere to the concepts of [Islamic law](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia"). This form of banking revolves around several well-established principles based on Islamic laws. All banking activities must avoid interest, a concept that is forbidden in Islam. Instead, the bank earns profit ([markup](/wiki/Markup_(business) "Markup (business)")) and fees on the financing facilities that it extends to customers.

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: United States")]

Main article: [Banking in the United States](/wiki/Banking_in_the_United_States "Banking in the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/People%27s_Trust_Company_Building_183_Montague_Street_Brooklyn.jpg/250px-People%27s_Trust_Company_Building_183_Montague_Street_Brooklyn.jpg)](/wiki/File:People%27s_Trust_Company_Building_183_Montague_Street_Brooklyn.jpg)

[Citibank](/wiki/Citibank "Citibank"), The People's Trust Company Building, [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").

The United States banking industry is one of the most heavily regulated and guarded in the world,[[45]](#cite_note-45) with multiple specialized and focused regulators. All banks with FDIC-insured deposits have the [Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation](/wiki/Federal_Deposit_Insurance_Corporation "Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation") (FDIC) as a regulator. However, for soundness examinations (i.e., whether a bank is operating in a sound manner), the [Federal Reserve](/wiki/Federal_Reserve "Federal Reserve") is the primary federal regulator for Fed-member state banks; the [Office of the Comptroller of the Currency](/wiki/Office_of_the_Comptroller_of_the_Currency "Office of the Comptroller of the Currency") (OCC) is the primary federal regulator for national banks. State non-member banks are examined by the state agencies as well as the FDIC.[[46]](#cite_note-46): 236 National banks have one primary regulator – the OCC.

Each regulatory agency has its own set of rules and regulations to which banks and thrifts must adhere.
The [Federal Financial Institutions Examination Council](/wiki/Federal_Financial_Institutions_Examination_Council "Federal Financial Institutions Examination Council") (FFIEC) was established in 1979 as a formal inter-agency body empowered to prescribe uniform principles, standards, and report forms for the federal examination of financial institutions. Although the FFIEC has resulted in a greater degree of regulatory consistency between the agencies, the rules and regulations are constantly changing.

In addition to changing regulations, changes in the industry have led to consolidations within the Federal Reserve, FDIC, OTS, and OCC. Offices have been closed, supervisory regions have been merged, staff levels have been reduced and budgets have been cut. The remaining regulators face an increased burden with an increased workload and more banks per regulator. While banks struggle to keep up with the changes in the regulatory environment, regulators struggle to manage their workload and effectively regulate their banks. The impact of these changes is that banks are receiving less hands-on assessment by the regulators, less time spent with each institution, and the potential for more problems slipping through the cracks, potentially resulting in an overall increase in bank failures across the United States.

The changing economic environment has a significant impact on banks and thrifts as they struggle to effectively manage their interest rate spread in the face of low rates on loans, rate competition for deposits and the general market changes, industry trends and economic fluctuations. It has been a challenge for banks to effectively set their growth strategies with the recent economic market. A rising interest rate environment may seem to help financial institutions, but the effect of the changes on consumers and businesses is not predictable and the challenge remains for banks to grow and effectively manage the spread to generate a return to their shareholders.

The management of the banks' asset portfolios also remains a challenge in today's economic environment. Loans are a bank's primary asset category and when loan quality becomes suspect, the foundation of a bank is shaken to the core. While always an issue for banks, declining [asset quality](/wiki/Asset_quality "Asset quality") has become a big problem for financial institutions.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Mirror_building_%284004925857%29.jpg/250px-Mirror_building_%284004925857%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mirror_building_(4004925857).jpg)

[Safra National Bank](/w/index.php?title=Safra_National_Bank&action=edit&redlink=1 "Safra National Bank (page does not exist)"), New York

There are several reasons for this, one of which is the lax attitude some banks have adopted because of the years of "good times." The potential for this is exacerbated by the reduction in the regulatory oversight of banks and in some cases depth of management. Problems are more likely to go undetected, resulting in a significant impact on the bank when they are discovered. In addition, banks, like any business, struggle to cut costs and have consequently eliminated certain expenses, such as adequate employee training programs.

Banks also face a host of other challenges such as aging ownership groups. Across the country, many banks' management teams and boards of directors are aging. Banks also face ongoing pressure from shareholders, both public and private, to achieve earnings and growth projections. Regulators place added pressure on banks to manage the various categories of risk. Banking is also an extremely competitive industry. Competing in the financial services industry has become tougher with the entrance of such players as insurance agencies, credit unions, check cashing services, [credit card](/wiki/Credit_card "Credit card") companies, etc.

As a reaction, banks have developed their activities in [financial instruments](/wiki/Financial_instruments "Financial instruments"), through [financial market](/wiki/Financial_market "Financial market") operations such as [brokerage](/wiki/Brokerage_firm "Brokerage firm") and have become big players in such activities.

Another major challenge is the aging infrastructure, also called legacy IT. Backend systems were built decades ago and are incompatible with new applications. Fixing bugs and creating interfaces costs huge sums, as knowledgeable programmers become scarce.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

### Loan activities of banks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Loan activities of banks")]

To be able to provide home buyers and builders with the funds needed, banks must compete for deposits. The phenomenon of [disintermediation](/wiki/Disintermediation "Disintermediation") had to dollars moving from savings accounts and into direct market instruments such as [U.S. Department of Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury "United States Department of the Treasury") obligations, agency securities, and corporate debt. One of the greatest factors in recent years in the movement of deposits was the tremendous growth of money market funds whose higher interest rates attracted consumer deposits.[[48]](#cite_note-multiple3-48)

To compete for deposits, US savings institutions offer many different types of plans:[[48]](#cite_note-multiple3-48)

* [Passbook](/wiki/Passbook "Passbook") or ordinary [deposit accounts](/wiki/Deposit_account "Deposit account")  – permit any amount to be added to or withdrawn from the account at any time.
* NOW and Super NOW accounts  - function like checking accounts but earn interest. A minimum balance may be required on Super NOW accounts.
* [Money market accounts](/wiki/Money_market_account "Money market account")  - carry a monthly limit of preauthorized transfers to other accounts or persons and may require a minimum or average balance.
* Certificate accounts  – subject to loss of some or all interest on withdrawals before maturity.
* Notice accounts  – the equivalent of certificate accounts with an indefinite term. Savers agree to notify the institution a specified time before withdrawal.
* [Individual retirement accounts](/wiki/Individual_retirement_account "Individual retirement account") (IRAs) and [Keogh plans](/wiki/Keogh_plan "Keogh plan")  – a form of retirement savings in which the funds deposited and interest earned are exempt from income tax until after withdrawal.
* [Checking accounts](/wiki/Checking_account "Checking account")  – offered by some institutions under definite restrictions.
* All withdrawals and deposits are completely the sole decision and responsibility of the account owner unless the parent or guardian is required to do otherwise for legal reasons.
* Club accounts and other [savings accounts](/wiki/Savings_account "Savings account")  – designed to help people save regularly to meet certain goals.

### Investment for the fossil fuel industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Investment for the fossil fuel industry")]

Even after The [Paris Agreement](/wiki/Paris_Agreement "Paris Agreement") has entered into force, large banks in [developed countries](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country") are heavily financing the [fossil fuel](/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel") industry. This practice has been condemned as going against [climate action](/wiki/Climate_action "Climate action").[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)

## Types of accounts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Types of accounts")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/BBTLexington.jpg/250px-BBTLexington.jpg)](/wiki/File:BBTLexington.jpg)

Suburban bank branch

[Bank statements](/wiki/Bank_statement "Bank statement") are accounting records produced by banks under the various accounting standards of the world. Under [GAAP](/wiki/Generally_accepted_accounting_principles "Generally accepted accounting principles") there are two kinds of accounts: debit and credit. Credit accounts are Revenue, Equity and Liabilities. Debit Accounts are Assets and Expenses. The bank credits a *credit account* to increase its balance, and debits a *credit account* to decrease its balance.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

The customer debits his or her savings/bank (asset) in his ledger when making a deposit (and the account is normally in debit), while the customer credits a [credit card](/wiki/Credit_card "Credit card") (liability) account in his ledger every time he spends money (and the account is normally in credit). When the customer reads his bank statement, the statement will show a credit to the account for deposits, and debits for withdrawals of funds. The customer with a positive balance will see this balance reflected as a credit balance on the bank statement. If the customer is overdrawn, he will have a negative balance, reflected as a debit balance on the bank statement.

### Brokered deposits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Brokered deposits")]

One source of deposits for banks is **deposit brokers** who deposit large sums of money on behalf of investors through trust corporations. This money will generally go to the banks which offer the most favorable terms, often better than those offered local depositors. It is possible for a bank to engage in business with no local deposits at all, all funds being **brokered deposits**. Accepting a significant quantity of such deposits, or "[hot money](/wiki/Hot_money "Hot money")" as it is sometimes called, puts a bank in a difficult and sometimes risky position, as the funds must be lent or invested in a way that yields a return sufficient to pay the high interest being paid on the brokered deposits. This may result in risky decisions and even in eventual failure of the bank. Banks that failed in the United States during the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis") had, on average, four times more brokered deposits as a percent of their deposits than the average bank. Such deposits, combined with risky real estate investments, factored into the [savings and loan crisis](/wiki/Savings_and_loan_crisis "Savings and loan crisis") of the 1980s. Regulation of brokered deposits is opposed by banks on the grounds that the practice can be a source of external funding to growing communities with insufficient local deposits.[[52]](#cite_note-52) There are different types of accounts: saving, recurring and current accounts.

### Custodial accounts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Custodial accounts")]

Custodial accounts are accounts in which assets are held for a third party. For example, businesses that accept custody of funds for clients prior to their conversion, return, or transfer may have a custodial account at a bank for these purposes.

## Globalization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bank&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Globalization")]

Further information: [Financial integration](/wiki/Financial_integration "Financial integration")

In modern times there have been huge reductions to the barriers of global competition in the banking industry. Increases in telecommunications and other financial technologies, such as [Bloomberg](/wiki/Bloomberg_Terminal "Bloomberg Terminal"), have allowed banks to extend their reach all over the world since they no longer have to be near customers to manage both their finances and their risk. The growth in cross-border activities has also increased the demand for banks that can provide various services across borders to different nationalities. Despite these reductions in barriers and growth in cross-border activities, the banking industry is nowhere near as globalized as some other industries. In the US, for instance, very few banks even worry about the Riegle–Neal Act, which promotes more efficient interstate banking. In the vast majority of nations around the globe, the market share for foreign owned banks is currently less than a tenth of all market shares for banks in a particular nation. One reason the banking industry has not been fully globalized is that it is more convenient to have local banks provide loans to small businesses and individuals. On the other hand, for large corporations, it is not as important in what nation the bank is in since the corporation's financial information is available around the globe.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

There have been two significant attempts to overcome the industry's traditional focus on competing at the national level rather than the international level. In the 1980s, [Citigroup](/wiki/Citigroup "Citigroup") and [HSBC](/wiki/HSBC "HSBC") both began to develop large networks of retail bank branches in numerous countries around the world, in order to become global consumer banking brands. But in 2021, Citigroup initiated an exit from retail banking outside of its core U.S. market,[[54]](#cite_note-Moise-54) while in 2022, HSBC initiated an exit from the U.S. retail market (except for its [wealth management](/wiki/Wealth_management "Wealth management") business)[[55]](#cite_note-Yoon-55) and then in 2023 put its retail operations in a dozen other countries under review for sale or closure.[[56]](#cite_note-White-56) According to [Wells Fargo](/wiki/Wells_Fargo "Wells Fargo"), both banks were operating on the assumption that globalization would lead to the rise of large numbers of consumers who would regularly travel across borders for both work and play, but that "global consumer customer never materialized".[[54]](#cite_note-Moise-54)
